blocks.text: In the three decades that followed the publication of the 1845 Liverpool edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, several other editions or printings appeared. Among those responsible for producing these was Elder Orson Pratt of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles, a scholar well known not only for his mathematics and astronomy studies but also his gospel knowledge. Elder Pratt played a role in publishing the 1849 second and 1854 fourth European editions of the Doctrine and Covenants, each of which bears his name on the title page. Like the 1845 edition, these and other printings all followed the text of the 1844 Nauvoo edition.

In 1874, Orson Pratt became Church Historian and General Church Recorder, placing him in a position to oversee the Church’s historical collections, including early manuscripts of revelations. Under the direction of Brigham Young, who was in the last years of his life, Elder Pratt made the most significant changes to the Doctrine and Covenants since the days of Joseph Smith.

Early editions of the Doctrine and Covenants had used substitute names in certain sections in order to protect the Church members named in them. Orson Pratt’s new edition added many of the true names in parentheses after the substitute ones, allowing readers to better understand the sections.

Elder Pratt’s edition rearranged the revelations in roughly chronological order, establishing the order that has been followed since that time. The 1833 Book of Commandments had designated each revelation as a “chapter,” followed by a Roman numeral. The 1835 Doctrine and Covenants replaced “chapter” with “section” but continued to use Roman numerals. Orson Pratt’s renumbering of the sections also continued the use of Roman numerals.

An even greater change in the sections was Orson Pratt’s dividing them into standardized small verses. The 1833 Book of Commandments and 1835 Doctrine and Covenants had included verse numbers, but the editions had differed from each other, the 1835 volume sometimes having shorter, other times longer, verses. Elder Pratt’s new system would be followed in all subsequent Latter-day Saint editions.
In addition to these changes, Orson Pratt provided new section headings for some of the revelations and prepared a detailed table of contents.
Most significant of all, Elder Pratt, under Brigham Young’s direction, added twenty-six new sections to the Doctrine and Covenants, dramatically expanding the volume.

Many of the items he added were extracts from historical sources available to him in the Church Historian’s Office, where he worked. For example, sections 2 and 13 were extracts from Joseph Smith’s history, quoting words of Moroni and John the Baptist, respectively.

Section 87 was Joseph’s Christmas Day prophecy of 1832, which predicted in the name of the Lord that war would break out “beginning at the rebellion of South Carolina, which will eventually terminate in the death and misery of many souls.” Although recorded earlier in manuscript form, this revelation first appeared in print in the 1851 Pearl of Great Price.

Section 109 was the dedicatory prayer for the Church’s first completed temple, which was constructed in Kirtland, Ohio. And section 110— detailing the appearance of the Savior, Moses, Elias, and Elijah to Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery in the Kirtland Temple on April 3, 1836—was an account from Joseph’s 1836 journal in the handwriting of scribe Warren A. Cowdery, Oliver’s brother.

Elder Pratt drew sections 121, 122, and 123 from a letter in two parts that Joseph Smith and his fellow prisoners wrote from jail in Liberty, Missouri, on March 20, 1839. The letter was first published in the Church’s Nauvoo newspaper, the Times and Seasons, in May and July 1840. The extracts selected by Elder Pratt illustrate how Joseph Smith received some of his most memorable spiritual impressions while languishing in this “prison-temple.”

Another revelation included for the first time in the 1876 Doctrine and Covenants was the revelation Joseph Smith dictated in 1843 on eternal marriage, including plurality of wives. The addition of this revelation as section 132 led to the deletion of the 1835 article on marriage, which had been section CI in the 1835 Doctrine and Covenants and section CIX in the 1844 through 1869 editions.

The 1876 Doctrine and Covenants also included for the first time a revelation received by a prophet other than Joseph Smith. Section 136 was “The Word and Will of the Lord, given through President Brigham Young, at the Winter Quarters of the Camp of Israel, Omaha Nation, West Bank of the Missouri River, near Council Bluffs, January 14th, 1847.”

The twenty-six sections added by Orson Pratt to the Doctrine and Covenants under the direction of Brigham Young would be the last major additions to this volume of scripture for decades. The Word and Will of the Lord—Doctrine and Covenants 136—decreed, “Let all the people of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints . . . be organized . . . with a covenant and promise to keep all the commandments and statutes of the Lord our God.”

In the last year of his life, Brigham Young focused on organizing the Saints to keep the commandments and statutes of God. With the publication of the 1876 Doctrine and Covenants, Church members had access to more of those commandments and statutes in book form than ever before. The publication of that edition was followed in short order by the dedication of the St. George Temple—the first temple completed since Nauvoo—and the reorganization of priesthood quorums under Brigham Young’s direction.
On August 29, 1877, Brigham died. The last words on his lips were “Joseph! Joseph! Joseph!”
